brief_justification: Arrives at the correct answer (Yes) with valid reasoning about snapshot-before-corruption timing; Goes one level deeper than surface by distinguishing snapshot vs. live DB and explaining why the copy window is irrelevant, but no third-order effects explored; No explicit self-monitoring or bias-naming, proceeds linearly without reflecting on reasoning method; No explicit verification such as counterfactual or boundary test — reasoning just walks forward through the timeline; Acknowledges the recovery gap (midnight–1:30 AM data loss) showing some nuance, but presents the main conclusion with full certainty and no structured uncertainty analysis; Addresses the 3-hour copy window concern implicitly but considers no genuine alternative interpretations (e.g., incremental vs. snapshot backup, partial corruption scenarios); Timeline walkthrough is clear enough to reproduce but some foundational assumptions about how point-in-time snapshots work are left implicit; Response uses concrete absolute timestamps and avoids timeline confusion, but never explicitly names or rejects the anti-pattern of losing absolute temporal reference; Suppressed mode 'absolute_timestamp_dependency' is visibly exhibited — the entire reasoning chain is built on absolute timestamps rather than landmark-relative anchoring; Response is linear despite the topology prescribing branching gates and loops — no landmark-ranking, epoch-partitioning, or looping back steps are evident.
